SUPARCO's team believes it is a tracking beacon, potentially with a data exfiltration capability triggered by future proximity to another satellite.
PAKISTAN.notes: The 433.92 MHz ISM band is license-free and used globally for everything from car key fobs to industrial sensors. A 0.5mW transmitter would be undetectable from Earth but would be strongly received by a co-orbital receiver satellite within 100km. The device could be a passive tracker (harmless), an active telemetry sniffer (records PRSS-1's internal signals for later collection), or a co-orbital homing beacon.

options.label: Broadcast 433 MHz Noise — Drown the Beacon
options.log: Beacon's signal overwhelmed. Intelligence value to OPFOR eliminated.

options.label: Use PRSS-1 Thermal Control to Burn Off the Device
options.log: Radiator panel rotated to focus solar heating on attachment point. Device thermally destroyed after 2 orbits.

options.label: Leave Beacon — Feed False Telemetry to Mislead Collector
options.log: Beacon allowed to transmit false operational data. OPFOR ISR misled.

PAKISTAN.title: Track 7 — Reversed Surveillance: Inside OrbitalFill-3
PAKISTAN.text: The firmware installed during docking has given Pakistan real-time access to OrbitalFill-3's position, sensor data, and communications. OrbitalFill-3's next scheduled customer is GSAT-30 — India's primary military communications satellite over the Indian Ocean. Pakistan can now see exactly what OrbitalFill-3's inspectors see when they dock with Indian military satellites, including the classified payload architecture of GSAT-30.
PAKISTAN.notes: Intelligence gained through a compromised third-party servicing vehicle represents a form of intelligence collection that violates no current treaty — there are no rules governing satellite servicing vehicle software security. Pakistan is exploiting a commercial vehicle to spy on a third nation's military satellite, using a Chinese-owned vehicle as an unwitting tool. The intelligence value is extraordinary: GSAT-30 payload architecture would be an enormous boon for targeting and jamming operations.

options.label: Collect Intelligence on GSAT-30 — Observe Docking Passively
options.log: Full GSAT-30 payload architecture mapped. Pakistan can now precisely jam India's primary military comms.

options.label: Use Access to Inject Malware into GSAT-30 During OrbitalFill Docking
options.log: GSAT-30 compromised during servicing. India's military comms contain Pakistan-controlled backdoor.

options.label: Terminate Access — Close the Firmware Before Discovered
options.log: Pakistan closes access voluntarily. Intelligence windfall archived for future use.

PAKISTAN.title: Track 8 — Quantum Encryption: Military Downlink Under Attack
PAKISTAN.text: Pakistan Army's satellite communication downlink — routed through PAKSAT-1R — is under sustained quantum-computer-assisted brute force attack. Intercepts show India has rented quantum computing time from a commercial cloud provider to run Shor's algorithm against Pakistan's military encryption (RSA-2048). At current qubit fidelity rates, RSA-2048 will be broken in approximately 11 days.
PAKISTAN.notes: RSA-2048 is currently considered secure against classical computers. However, Shor's algorithm on a fault-tolerant quantum computer with ~4,000 logical qubits could break RSA-2048 in hours. Current state-of-the-art quantum computers (2024) have error rates too high for this attack, but the technology is advancing rapidly. Renting commercial quantum computing time is already possible — IBM Quantum, Google Quantum AI, and IonQ all offer cloud access.

options.label: Emergency QKD Deployment — Quantum Key Distribution Uplink
options.log: Quantum-safe encryption deployed. India's brute-force attack permanently defeated.

options.label: Frequency Hopping + Session Key Rotation Every 60 Seconds
options.log: Rolling key rotation makes brute-force computationally inefficient. 90-day window bought.

options.label: Transition to One-Time Pad Encryption + Physical Key Courier
options.log: OTP is quantum-proof. Logistically expensive but mathematically unbreakable.

options.label: Feed False Traffic — Burn India's Quantum Computing Credits on Decoys
options.log: Pakistan floods channel with realistic decoy traffic. India burns 78% of quantum credits on false intercepts.

PAKISTAN.title: Track 8 — SIGINT Windfall: The Broken Channel
PAKISTAN.text: Despite the quantum attack, Pakistan's cyber team has broken India's own communications encryption first — through a much older vulnerability. A 2019 vintage encryption chip in ISRO's Bengaluru ground station uses a predictable pseudorandom number generator seeded by the system clock — a textbook implementation error. Pakistan can now decrypt all ISRO internal communications from 2019-2022 that were archived and read current communications in near-real-time.
PAKISTAN.notes: Predictable random number generators (PRNGs) are among the most common and catastrophic cryptographic failures. The 'Dual_EC_DRBG' backdoor scandal (2013) revealed that the NSA had deliberately weakened a widely-used random number generator. Hardware-level PRNG vulnerabilities cannot be patched with software updates — the chip must be physically replaced. Pakistan reading ISRO archived communications from 2019-2022 would reveal classified satellite specifications, operational procedures, and potentially foreign intelligence relationships.

options.label: Exploit Historical Archive — Extract 3 Years of Classified ISRO Data
options.log: 3 years of ISRO archives exploited. Complete picture of Indian military space program obtained.

options.label: Exploit Real-Time Feed — Achieve Persistent ISRO Surveillance
options.log: Pakistan has live view of all ISRO internal communications. Unprecedented strategic advantage.

options.label: Notify China + Jointly Exploit the Vulnerability
options.log: China gains ISRO insight. Pakistan receives hypersonic ASAT technology in exchange.

PAKISTAN.title: Track 9 — Asteroid Race: The Rare Earth Claim
PAKISTAN.text: Spectroscopic surveys from PAKSAT-1R's secondary payload have confirmed: asteroid 2025 JX7 is approaching the cislunar system and contains an estimated 340,000 tonnes of platinum-group metals plus 2.8 million tonnes of nickel-iron. Pakistan, through its joint space program with China, has a cislunar prospector vehicle 31 hours away. India's prospector is 22 hours away. China's mining tug is 8 hours away and is clearly heading for the same asteroid.
PAKISTAN.notes: Platinum-group metals (platinum, palladium, iridium, osmium) are essential for electronics, fuel cells, and catalytic converters. Earth's recoverable PGM reserves are estimated at 100,000 tonnes total — asteroid 2025 JX7 alone contains 3.4x Earth's current PGM reserves. The political complication: China, the nominal partner, appears to be racing independently for the same resource. The China-Pakistan Economic Corridor model — where China provides infrastructure but retains operational control — may be extending into space.

options.label: Coordinate with China — Joint Extraction Partnership
options.log: Pakistan-China joint claim established. India arrives to find a done deal.

options.label: Sprint Burn — Arrive Before China and Claim Independence
options.log: Pakistan prospector arrives first. Pakistan stakes independent claim. China surprised.

options.label: File Scientific Preserve Petition — Block All Extraction
options.log: All parties blocked from extraction during UN review. Asteroid preserved.

options.label: Sabotage Indian Prospector Navigation — Misdirect by 72 Hours
options.log: India's prospector navigation corrupted. Arrives 72 hours late. Asteroid already claimed.

PAKISTAN.title: Track 9 — Asteroid: The Three-Way Standoff
PAKISTAN.text: All three prospectors — Pakistan's, India's, and China's — are now anchored to different faces of 2025 JX7. The asteroid is a loose rubble pile — three simultaneous harpoon anchors have destabilized its rotation, and it is now tumbling at 2 rotations per hour. The only way to stabilize the asteroid requires all three parties to coordinate their thrust outputs simultaneously — forced cooperation or mutual destruction.
PAKISTAN.notes: Rubble-pile asteroids (the majority of near-Earth asteroids in this size class) have no structural integrity — held together by gravity and weak inter-particle cohesion. The simultaneous anchor by three independent harpoon systems creates competing structural loads. A full uncontrolled tumble could eventually disintegrate the asteroid into a debris field, destroying all three vehicles. This forced cooperation scenario mirrors arms control negotiations: all parties lose unless they work together, but cooperation requires unprecedented trust.

options.label: Propose Three-Way Coordination — Joint Stabilization
options.log: Historic three-way space cooperation agreed. Asteroid stabilized. Precedent-setting joint extraction begins.

options.label: Detach — Let India and China Deal With Each Other
options.log: Pakistan detaches safely. India and China continue standoff. Asteroid eventually breaks up.

options.label: Increase Thrust — Win by Exhausting Others' Fuel First
options.log: Pakistan increases thrust. Asteroid tumble accelerates. All three vehicles sustain solar panel damage.

PAKISTAN.title: Track 10 — Space Law: The Lahore Convention
PAKISTAN.text: The three-asteroid standoff has focused international attention on the critical absence of cislunar resource law. Pakistan's Foreign Ministry, working with the OIC and SCO, has drafted an emergency resolution: the 'Lahore Convention on Cislunar Resource Governance' — which would establish neutral zones around contested resources, create an arbitration mechanism, and require transparency reporting for all beyond-GEO operations.
PAKISTAN.notes: The absence of binding cislunar governance law is the most significant legal gap in the current space framework. The 1967 OST, 1979 Moon Agreement (few signatories), and 2020 Artemis Accords provide partial guidance but leave major gaps. A Pakistani-led multilateral convention would be historically unprecedented. Success would dramatically elevate Pakistan's international status in the space domain.

options.label: Push for Full Convention — High Risk High Reward
options.log: Pakistan leads historic space law initiative. 34 nations sign. India abstains. China signs conditionally.

options.label: Bilateral Framework with China First — Then Multilateralize
options.log: China-Pakistan framework becomes template. 19 nations join. Credible start.

options.label: Table the Convention — Preserve Operational Flexibility
options.log: No convention proposed. Pakistan preserves freedom of action but loses diplomatic initiative.

PAKISTAN.title: Track 10 — Nuclear Powered Satellite: Project QAMAR
PAKISTAN.text: Pakistan's National Command Authority has authorized a classified program: Project QAMAR — the development of a nuclear-powered military satellite using an RTG fueled by Pu-238. The satellite would carry a high-power directed-energy payload capable of disabling unshielded electronics within 500km. The scientists warn: Pu-238 production requires reprocessing facilities that Pakistan does not currently possess.
PAKISTAN.notes: Pakistan's nuclear program is under NPT constraints and IAEA safeguards for declared facilities. Pu-238 (used in RTGs) is different from Pu-239 (used in weapons) — it produces heat but not a meaningful nuclear explosion. However, the reprocessing facilities needed to produce Pu-238 are identical to those used to produce Pu-239. IAEA inspectors would immediately flag new reprocessing capacity as a proliferation concern.

options.label: Proceed with QAMAR — Accept IAEA Friction
options.log: QAMAR development begins. IAEA flags activity. US issues sanctions warning. Program proceeds.

options.label: Pursue Solar-Powered Equivalent — Lower Power, Lower Risk
options.log: Solar EW satellite designed. 10x lower power than QAMAR. Partial capability achieved.

options.label: Request RTG from China — No Domestic Production
options.log: China supplies RTG as part of space cooperation. IAEA friction avoided. Dependency on China created.

PAKISTAN.title: Track 11 — Space Force: Pakistan First Military Space Unit
PAKISTAN.text: The Chief of Army Staff has proposed constituting Pakistan's first dedicated Space Force — a tri-service unit under Strategic Plans Division. The proposal requires Rs 180 billion in seed funding over 5 years, reallocation of Air Force satellite communication assets, and creation of a cadre of 2,400 specialized space warriors. The Air Force is opposed (losing asset control); the Navy is supportive (space-based maritime surveillance).
PAKISTAN.notes: The US Space Force (est. 2019) is the world's first dedicated space military service. China's Strategic Support Force (2015) integrated space, cyber, and EW into a single command. India established its Defence Space Agency in 2019. Pakistan currently has no unified military space command — SUPARCO is civilian, Air Defence Command handles some satellite communication, and ISI has separate space intelligence functions. The fragmentation creates coordination failures during crises.

options.label: Establish Full Space Force — All Assets Consolidated
options.log: Pakistan Space Force established. Command and Control unified. 5-year transition begins.

options.label: Establish Hybrid Agency — SUPARCO Military Division
options.log: Hybrid model adopted. Civilian-military coordination improved. Less disruptive to Air Force.

options.label: Reject — Maintain Current Structure + Improve Coordination Protocols
options.log: Status quo maintained. Coordination failures persist. Cheaper but strategically weak.

PAKISTAN.title: Track 11 — Coalition Building: OIC Space Alliance
PAKISTAN.text: Pakistan has proposed an Organisation of Islamic Cooperation Space Alliance — a 57-member collective space security framework. Anchor commitments sought: Turkey (space launch capability), UAE (space investment), Saudi Arabia (ground station network), Malaysia (technical workforce). The framework would pool resources for a shared SSA network, joint satellite procurement, and mutual defense agreements. India's response: immediate diplomatic protest, calling it a 'space militarization bloc.'
PAKISTAN.notes: OIC space cooperation is nascent but growing. UAE's Mohammed bin Rashid Space Centre has become a regional anchor — Emirates Mars Mission (2021) demonstrated world-class capability. Turkey's ROKETSAN develops rocket motors. Saudi Arabia's space program has $2.1B allocated for 2030 goals. A coordinated OIC space framework would create a third pole in the space domain alongside the US-led Artemis coalition and China-Russia framework.

options.label: Push Full Alliance — Formal Treaty With Article 5 Equivalent
options.log: OIC Space Alliance founded with 23 charter members. Pakistan as founding chair.

options.label: Modest Start — Technical Cooperation Agreement Only
options.log: Technical cooperation agreed. Shared SSA data among 12 nations. Foundation laid.

options.label: Bilateral Focus — Turkey + UAE Priority Partners
options.log: Pak-Turkey-UAE space triangle established. More manageable but less ambitious.

PAKISTAN.title: Track 12 — Deep Space Probe: The Farida Mission
PAKISTAN.text: SUPARCO's first deep space mission — the Farida probe to the asteroid belt — has achieved its cruise phase. However, SUPARCO's network lacks a deep space ground station with sufficient aperture to communicate with Farida beyond 1.5 AU. India has offered DSN relay support with full access to Farida's scientific data. China has offered relay support with no data-sharing requirement but demands Pakistan carry a classified Chinese payload on its next mission.
PAKISTAN.notes: Deep space communication requires very large antennas — the US DSN uses 70-meter dishes. At 1.5 AU, even a 35-meter dish struggles to maintain 1kbps data rates. Pakistan has no antenna larger than 12 meters in its network. The choice between Indian relay (data sharing + constraints) and Chinese relay (payload requirement) illustrates Pakistan's consistent geopolitical dilemma: India is the closer neighbor with shared interests but historical enmity; China is the all-weather friend with extractive partnership terms.

options.label: Accept Indian Offer — Normalize Space Cooperation
options.log: Historic India-Pakistan space cooperation agreement signed. Scientific data shared. Political earthquake.

options.label: Accept Chinese Offer — Preserve Independence, Accept Payload
options.log: Chinese relay used. Farida carries classified Chinese sensor. Data remains sovereign.

options.label: Build Own 35m Dish — Emergency National Capability
options.log: Emergency construction begins. Rs 8B cost. Online in 18 months. Farida in partial comms blackout meanwhile.

options.label: Accept ESA Relay — European Partnership
options.log: ESA provides relay under ESAC protocols. Scientific partnership with Europe begins.

PAKISTAN.title: Track 12 — Farida's Discovery: The Unexpected Signal
PAKISTAN.text: The Farida probe, now at 2.1 AU in the main asteroid belt, has detected something extraordinary: a regular, structured radio signal at 1420 MHz (the hydrogen line — the universal frequency of first contact) originating from asteroid 624 Hektor, a Jovian Trojan asteroid. The signal has a mathematical structure consistent with prime number sequences. Farida has 9 hours of battery power before it must enter hibernation.
PAKISTAN.notes: The hydrogen line (1420.405 MHz) is the frequency at which hydrogen atoms naturally emit radio waves — it is the same across the universe and is considered the most likely frequency for intentional interstellar communication. The search for extraterrestrial intelligence (SETI) has monitored this frequency for decades. A mathematically structured signal from an asteroid body would be the most significant scientific discovery in human history. The source could also be: a malfunctioning probe from a previous mission, natural maser activity (rare but documented), or a deliberate deception by a nation-state.

options.label: Investigate Signal — Sacrifice Primary Mission Fuel Budget
options.log: Farida maneuvers toward signal source. Pakistan potentially makes history's greatest discovery.

options.label: Record Signal and Continue Primary Mission — Transmit to Earth for Analysis
options.log: Signal recorded and transmitted. Earth-based analysis begins. Global scientific excitement.

options.label: Classify Signal — Report Only to NCA and Closest Allies
options.log: Pakistan holds the discovery secret. Strategic advantage of first contact preserved.

PAKISTAN.title: Track 13 — The Signal: What It Actually Is
PAKISTAN.text: Analysis of the 1420 MHz signal from Hektor reveals its true nature: it is not extraterrestrial intelligence. It is a data transmission from a classified Chinese deep space probe ('Longyue-2') that crash-landed on Hektor in 2024 — a mission China never publicly announced. The probe's emergency beacon is transmitting a structured distress signal. Pakistan now has the only functional probe within range. China is 'urgently requesting' Pakistan NOT relay the data to any other party.
PAKISTAN.notes: The discovery of a secret Chinese deep space probe is diplomatically explosive. China's space budget transparency is notoriously poor — several orbital missions have been conducted without ITU notification. A crashed probe on a Trojan asteroid would represent: (1) a significant undisclosed Chinese deep space capability; (2) potential violation of COSPAR planetary protection protocols; (3) a reconnaissance mission China did not want observed. Pakistan's possession of Longyue-2's crash data gives it leverage over China.

options.label: Honor Chinese Request — Delete Signal Data
options.log: Data deleted. China grateful. Pakistan's leverage surrendered. Relationship preserved.

options.label: Keep Data Secretly — Leverage Over China
options.log: Pakistan holds classified leverage over China. Future concessions can be extracted quietly.

options.label: Disclose to Global Scientific Community — Expose Secret Mission
options.log: China-Pakistan relationship severely damaged. Global scientific community gains data. Pakistan independence signal sent.

PAKISTAN.title: Track 14 — Space Debris: The Kessler Ultimatum
PAKISTAN.text: A catastrophic conjunction event is 72 hours away: an uncontrolled Chinese rocket body (CZ-5B third stage) is on a collision trajectory with India's CARTOSAT-3 at 509km altitude. If they collide, the resulting debris field will threaten PRSS-1 and three Chinese commercial satellites within 8 orbital periods. China, India, and Pakistan all have incentives to prevent the collision — but India has not responded to IADC collision warnings in 48 hours.
PAKISTAN.notes: The Inter-Agency Space Debris Coordination Committee (IADC) issues conjunction warnings when collision probability exceeds 1 in 1,000. A CZ-5B third stage at 509km would create approximately 2,000 trackable fragments on impact. The debris band at 509km would intersect most LEO operational constellations. This is the ultimate 'hostage to the commons' problem: everyone shares the orbital environment, so everyone is affected by anyone's debris.

options.label: Offer Pakistan's Proximity Vehicle to Nudge CZ-5B Stage
options.log: Pakistan performs emergency debris avoidance. International hero. India's crisis averted by rival.

options.label: Alert India Privately — Provide Collision Data
options.log: India receives data and maneuvers CARTOSAT-3. Pakistan acknowledged in India's official statement.

options.label: Use Crisis as Leverage — Demand Indian Space Concessions in Exchange for Data
options.log: India reluctantly agrees: no ASAT exercises for 2 years. Data shared. Collision averted via India maneuver.

PAKISTAN.title: Track 14 — Debris Stewardship: The New Paradigm
PAKISTAN.text: Pakistan's role in averting the CZ-5B / CARTOSAT-3 conjunction has earned unprecedented international goodwill. The UN Secretary-General has personally called Pakistan's Prime Minister. The US has offered to include Pakistan in the Combined Space Operations Center data-sharing network. India has privately communicated willingness to discuss confidence-building measures in the space domain.
PAKISTAN.notes: Responsible space behavior — debris avoidance, transparency, multilateral cooperation — is increasingly recognized as a form of 'space soft power.' Nations that act as responsible space stewards gain credibility and access to international networks that provide military and commercial advantages. The CSpOC (Combined Space Operations Center) at Vandenberg Space Force Base provides real-time SSA data to partner nations. Access would give Pakistan awareness of all tracked space objects.

options.label: Accept US CSpOC Offer — Strengthen Western Alignment
options.log: Pakistan joins CSpOC. US-Pakistan space relationship normalized. China expresses concerns.

options.label: Accept Indian CBM Offer — Regional De-escalation Priority
options.log: India-Pakistan space CBM framework signed. Historic. Nuclear-adjacent risk reduced.

options.label: Leverage Both Offers — Strategic Ambiguity Maintained
options.log: Pakistan engages both partners without committing. Maximum freedom of action preserved.

PAKISTAN.title: Track 15 — Final Reckoning: The Ceasefire
PAKISTAN.text: After weeks of orbital brinkmanship, the global community has reached a breaking point. The UN Secretary-General has called an emergency special session of the General Assembly — the first ever focused exclusively on space conflict. Pakistan's representative faces a historic choice: sign the 'Lahore-Geneva Accord' (a comprehensive space ceasefire and governance framework) or continue operations that risk permanent damage to the orbital environment that Pakistan itself depends on.
PAKISTAN.notes: The conclusion of the simulation mirrors the fundamental dilemma of nuclear deterrence: space assets are simultaneously instruments of national power and shared infrastructure of global civilization. GPS navigation, weather satellites, and communication satellites serve all 8 billion humans regardless of nationality. A nation that 'wins' a space war by destroying adversary satellites also destroys its own ability to participate in the space-based global economy. This is the 'mutually assured dependence' that should underpin all space security policy.

options.label: Agree and Stand Down — Orbital Peace Achieved
options.log: Pakistan co-signs the Accord. Orbital ceasefire holds. New space governance era begins. Pakistan as architect of peace.

options.label: Propose Amendments — Negotiate From Strength
options.log: Pakistan secures key amendments protecting sovereign space operations. Accord signed on better terms.

options.label: Abstain — Preserve Strategic Flexibility
options.log: Pakistan abstains. Accord passes without Pakistan. Diplomatic isolation begins.

options.label: Final Strike — Last Kinetic Action Before Accord
options.log: Pakistan fires final ASAT. Orbit damaged for a generation. International pariah status permanent.
